era_context: Continuation of the Middle Bronze Age. The Hyksos presence in Egypt was solidifying; Mesopotamia was fragmenting after Hammurabi's recent death; the Indus Valley Civilization remained in its mature phase; early Mycenaean palace culture was developing in Greece. This year falls within a period of significant demographic and political flux across the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East.

events.title: Continued Hyksos settlement in the Nile Delta
events.description: Archaeological evidence from Avaris and other Delta sites indicates ongoing Asiatic settlement and integration with the Egyptian population during this period. Material culture shows a blend of Egyptian and Levantine traditions. The Hyksos were establishing administrative centers and integrating into the existing Egyptian economic system, though they maintained distinct cultural practices and religious traditions.
events.certainty_note: The process of Hyksos settlement is archaeologically visible but not precisely dated to individual years. This entry represents the broader process occurring within a window of c. 1800–1650 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are documented for 1761 BCE in surviving sources. The assignment of Hyksos settlement to this year is conventional and not based on contemporary evidence. Modern scholarship increasingly questions whether the Hyksos represented a unified political entity or a gradual demographic shift.
historiographic_note: For 1761 BCE, there are no contemporary written sources that specifically document events of this year. The historical record is almost entirely dependent on archaeological inference and later compilations. This year is essentially a placeholder within a broader historical process. The absence of documentation is not unusual for this period; most years in the Bronze Age lack specific contemporary records.
